item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loved it, especially the diversity within the 8 tribes. Would almost upvote based on the premise of expansion by time period rather than physical land alone. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: [deleted]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I suggest we change the wording of the second to last line to
"When I reclaim my seventh tribe I'll cross the black divide"
to make it rhyme
This was my big comeback article after I deleted-and-remade my account- I'm glad to see that people liked it :).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm just now seeing this for the first time. This is really well done. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great use of time travel and culture-building.
Do any of the tribes have any contact with the Daevites?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perhaps it should be Thaumiel. They are, after all, containing a piece of the Foundation's possibly-greatest enemy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has got to be the most original use of time travel I have ever read. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I mostly agree - the ending reference feels like an unnecessary addition, with the article being just the right level of detail + hanging mystery without it. I may be biased by personally not liking Scarlet King stuff much, though.

Even with my ambivalence towards the ending, though, this is still one of my favourites, with a fantastic idea and smooth execution. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Their timeline is out of sync with ours, remember. Most of the tribes exist centuries in the past/future from when the events of 231 happened.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh. Each tribe forgot their duty as the 231 instances died, didn't they? And that's the one event they can't reverse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really great. An original concept, a good implementation and nice descriptions of the tribes; I've very few complaints. +1

I found redundancy in the second paragraph of the description, though.

Agreements and conflicts over the periods owned by each tribe are common, with sudden raids into a particular time period common.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This spooked the hell out of me

So it also deserves a +1 out of me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well 19th century Orientalists often made rather "loose" translations of Middle Eastern literature; Fitzgerald's famous translation of Omar Khayyam being a particularly famous example, which was practicaly as much his own poetry as it was Khayyam's. So the translation has been Anglicised a lot, and isn't exactly the same as the original, though the message is transmitted intact.

If I ever learn Turkmen then I'll add the original :P.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellently written and engaging. My only comment is how unlikely it would be that a poem written in Turkmen would keep meter and rhyme scheme when translated. +1 anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: εїз <3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The ending was pretty predictable, but the concept and buildup in this were all well done. It's always cool to see another Iranian history skip, especially one with a very weird history.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seven brides, Seven chains, Seven tribes, Seven gifts for the scarlet king. A madness unending, a demon brought forth, but in the end, all they thought was to send the thing north.
The demon lived, it continued to thrive, All this was thought of in it's fetid hive. It's mind unwavering, with the thought to hunt, All they were was but a grunt. It's chaos gave way to a plan, so it may become the end of man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yep, should be woven-shall edit.

item.title: Re: Admin Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Ah, thank you Zyn; that saves me a chore :).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tufto, your strong adherence to historical embeddedness has always impressed me, and this is no exception. While I do think the threat's a little trite and cops out by suggesting it instead of describing it (we've seen this before, in the article with the He-Who-Made-Dark meteorite, among others), it's enough for me to enjoy it for now. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Here ere is a poetic/archaic word meaning "before."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At this rate, I think we can weave the Scarlet King as a Turkic-Persian-Zoroastrian concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Re: image caption

Two members of SCP-3838-3, posing in front of a rug which they had weaved, 1908.

Woven?

item.title: Admin Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
jeez can you try to shrink down that image? It took my browser 2 minutes to fully load the page.

Agreed. Author, I shrunk the image down to 15% so it wouldn't be so huge. Feel free to edit as you see fit though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And ere the end of all your days the Crimson Khan shall ride.

typo?

A great scp nevertheless +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the concept, but tying in the Scarlet King at the end seems unnecessary for an article that could stand on its own with a teased-at mystery of what exactly the powerful figure/tribe is. This doesn't even add anything particularly compelling to the Scarlet King either, other than the self-contained intrigue of the nomads. Calling him the "Crimson Khan" in particular seems almost silly, like you just wanted to say something different and then opened up a dictionary. At the least I'd recommend just changing that to Scarlet King since it's not fooling anyone anyway (and there's a chance this could be someone's first introduction to the Scarlet King mythos, in which case googling "Crimson Khan" for more info would get them nowhere). But imho the more people try to incorporate what 231 established into their skip for extra intrigue or reference points the less compelling it makes 231 as its own thing

Still, definitely good enough to warrant an upvote. This was an excellent idea and executed very well

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconding this. Nicely done.

I also thought the envoy's combined urgency and barely restrained irritation was very well depicted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Unusually, they appear to follow strong Manichean beliefs, but associated "darkness" in Manichean dualism with the colour red.

This is when I should have realized where this was going, but I didn't catch it at the time. Nice foreshadowing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is utterly unique and fantastic.

Very strong +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hell of a concept and an excellent ending by tying in the Scarlet King mythos. It was cool to see some aspects of your old 001 and the original 3221 draft used to better effect here. Well done.

item.title: Damn.
item.content:encoded:
Kudos on your SCP. Very interesting and enjoyable to read.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the article, but jeez can you try to shrink down that image? It took my browser 2 minutes to fully load the page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very interesting concept of time periods being used as land, and I liked the descriptions of the different tribes in different timelines. The addenda were quite a nice addition also, really contextualized most of the article.

+1 very much.

also who doesn't love time traveling tribesmen

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Come gather 'round people
Wherever you roam
And admit that the waters
Around you have grown
And accept it that soon
You'll be drenched to the bone.
If your time to you
Is worth savin'
Then you better start swimmin'
Or you'll sink like a stone
For the times they are a-changin'.

With thanks to the following:

Balthaazar
Weryllium
Tuomey

The picture is in the public domain and can be found here.

You can find more of my stuff here.
